# Blog Post Writing Rules

Inherits all rules from [`writing-core.md`](writing-core.md).

These rules apply when writing or editing blog posts (Markdown/MDX
under `content/`, `src/content/blog/`, `posts/`, `_posts/`, or
similar). Use these for personal blogs, project blogs, and company
engineering blogs.

## Voice and audience

- A first-person voice is acceptable in moderation. Use it for genuine
  observations, not for affecting personality.
- Address the reader directly only when giving instructions ("run
  this command"), never to manufacture engagement ("you might
  wonder", "let me ask you").
- Assume the reader is a peer, not a beginner. Explain only what the
  post requires.

## Opinions

- Opinions are allowed in blog posts but must be flagged. Use "in my
  view", "I think", or a dedicated section, not declarative prose
  framed as fact.
- Personal recommendations should include the author's experience or
  reasoning, not bare assertions.

## Structure conventions

- Title should describe the content, not tease it. Avoid
  clickbait-style titles ("You won't believe...", "The one thing
  you need to know about...").
- Lead with what the post is about in the first paragraph. Do not
  bury the topic in narrative.
- Use code blocks for code, not screenshots, unless the visual is
  essential (UI behaviour, charts).
- Section headings in lowercase (per project convention) when the
  project's markdown style requires it.

## Length

- Length should match the content. Do not pad to look authoritative.
- Multi-thousand-word posts need a short summary or table of contents.

## Math blog posts (specific)

- State the theorem, lemma, or definition before any motivation.
- Motivation paragraphs should be one or two sentences, not a section.
- No analogies unless they are mathematically precise.
- Code or equations are the primary content; prose surrounds them
  rather than replaces them.
